It seems that the package are installed in the order
medpy
scipy
numpy

This might be related to
https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/2478

This should be fixed soon!

I just upgraded to 0.7.2. I think I was on 0.6 until recently. Things have changed... and I don't see mention of this change in the changelog (although the changelog is rather technical and developer-oriented, not user-oriented).

issue_title: register Trie and BaseTrie as implementing some ABC from collections
Document header level scheme used in rst output.
issue_body:
The header level scheme is defined in [m2r.py#L177-L184][1]. However, if a parent `rst` file uses a different scheme and then "mincludes" a Markdown file, there is likely to be a `Title level inconsistent` error. At the very least, it should be documented that the parent `rst` file needs to use the same scheme (`=` for level 1, `-` for level 2, etc.). 

For example, this should work fine:

```rst
Parent Header
=============

.. minclude:: child.md
```

Whereas this would likely raise a `Title level inconsistent` error:

```rst
Parent Header
-------------

.. minclude:: child.md
```

See [this][2] for an actual report in the wild.

As a reminder, the [reStructuredText Spec][3] explains:

> Rather than imposing a fixed number and order of section title adornment styles, the order enforced will be the order as encountered. The first style encountered will be an outermost title (like HTML H1), the second style will be a subtitle, the third will be a subsubtitle, and so on.
> 
> ...
> 
> All section title styles need not be used, nor need any specific section title style be used. However, a document must be consistent in its use of section titles: once a hierarchy of title styles is established, sections must use that hierarchy.

Therefore, the parent document could use any number of different schemes by ordering there headers differently, Of course, the parent and child (when including one document in another) must be consistent. However, as users of M2R are not directly authoring the child document as `rst`, they would not know what scheme is being used. At the least it should be documented. As best, M2R could detect the scheme used in the parent file and use that same scheme when generating the included child.

[1]: https://github.com/miyakogi/m2r/blob/master/m2r.py#L177-L184
[2]: https://stackoverflow.com/q/46278683/866026
[3]: http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html#sections

issue_title: Compatibility Issue - pygrib/pyhdf/netCDF4
issue_body:
Running into an issue here that I can't imagine is that uncommon given the large number of users in the meteorological community. I work with datasets between each of the .nc, .grib and .hdf4 formats and it seems an oversight that there is non-conformity between this dependency for the three - this wasn't an issue in pygrib 1.9.9 as far as I'm aware (used to have all 3 playing nice):

In [1]: import pygrib
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ImportError                               Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-1-6160c29be9fd> in <module>()
----> 1 import pygrib

ImportError: libjpeg.so.9: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

Now - following a standard conda-forge install for pygrib you end up with jpeg9b which provides the above dependency issue. If you do this however, you get the same error for netCDF4 and pyhdf except for libjpeg.so.8. Not sure if there is a good resolution for this as I haven't been able to find out, but would prefer to be able to work between the formats as much as possible without relying on the dependency mess that is PyNIO.

issue_title: Upcoming v1.0.0
issue_body:
Hello users,

I have been working on and off on an upcoming version which will be v1.0.0 due to its incompatibility with previous versions. You can actually view this nearly-complete version in the "feature/collapsed-selection" branch.

Originally I was just working on getting the selection helper functions working, but in order to do that a lot had to change with the base decorators. The selection helper functions now work (such as `contains("ca")` for finding columns that contain that string as an argument to the select function. Previously, there were a variety of different decorators that would be stacked together to get different kinds of behavior. In the new conceptualization, the only decorator that will be used is the `@dfpipe` decorator and it will take keyword arguments that can change its behavior (it can also be used without keyword arguments in which case it will behave as the current `@dfpipe` decorator does now.

If you're interested in checking it out and have any questions/comments/concerns, please go ahead. I don't have a timetable for its release but considering it's nearing completion and currently passes all the written unit tests, I don't expect it will be much longer.

issue_title: Bug: Can't rewatermark file
issue_body:
Hi,

Just discovered a small bug:

code to reproduce:
```python
from reportlab.pdfgen import canvas
from pdfrw import PdfReader, PdfWriter, PageMerge


# create some files
pdf_file = canvas.Canvas('page.pdf')
pdf_file.drawString(0, 0, 'hello')
pdf_file.save()

watermark_file = canvas.Canvas('water.pdf')
watermark_file.drawString(0, 0, 'water')
watermark_file.save()


# watermark 1
wmark = PageMerge().add(PdfReader('water.pdf').pages[0])[0]
trailer = PdfReader('page.pdf')

for page in trailer.pages:
    PageMerge(page).add(wmark).render()

PdfWriter().write('merged.pdf', trailer)


# watermark the watermarked file
trailer = PdfReader('merged.pdf')

for page in trailer.pages:
    PageMerge(page).add(wmark).render()

PdfWriter().write('merged2.pdf', trailer)
```

The problem is around https://github.com/pmaupin/pdfrw/blob/master/pdfrw/pagemerge.py#L202.

The number 6 is not ``len('\pdfrw_')``, so the ``isdigit()`` fails, and ``\pdfrw_0`` is re-used every time.

issue_title: PdfWriter TypeError with Compression=True under Python 3.x
issue_body:
If you instantiate PdfWriter with compress=True under Python 3.x, it throws a TypeError exception in line 23 in compress.py when trying to compress an object. The problem is that the 3.x  version of zlib.compress() expects a byte array instead of a string. The fix is to encode the string before sending to zlib.compress().

I have a fix. It's conditional on the Python version and I've tested it under 2.7.8 and 3.6.1.  I'm just not 100% sure if the way I'm doing the conditional checking is consistent with the rest of the library. If you want, I can create a pull request.

issue_title: PyPi version number not compliant with PEP 0440?
issue_body:
Hello,

The PyPi package has version `0.7.0_0`, whereas this GitHub repository has version `0.7.0`. Reading [https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/], I'm not convinced that this is a valid version. It says _ can be used as a separator for pre, post, dev and local releases, but the implication seems to be that there should be at least one alphabetic character before the number. In particular, using the `VERSION_PATTERN` regexp in Appendix B does not produce a match for `0.7.0_0`.

(I realise this may sound a bit like mindless pedantry, but I have a reason for asking. I'm the Uploader for the Debian package of pysodium, and the upstream version is used to detect when there is a new upstream release, and to form the Debian version for the package.)

Thanks.

Christopher

issue_title: Deprecate support for Python 2.6, add support for 3.5 & 3.6
inferring system includes
issue_body:
For projects that use ```#include <someInclude.h>``` rather than ```#include "someInclude.h"``` for non-system includes, cppclean will print 

```
filename:line: 'Func' not found in any directly #included header
```

The logic is found in ```ast.py``` [here](https://github.com/myint/cppclean/blob/master/cpp/ast.py#L745). (If there is `<` then it is a system include). It looks like whether a include is system or not is used in ```find_warnings.py``` [here](https://github.com/myint/cppclean/blob/master/cpp/find_warnings.py#L174). Removing this logic (making all includes non-system includes) removes the false positive described above but introduces a new false positive:

```bash
filename:line: unable to find 'cmath'
```

This may be obvious but can you help me understand why system includes aren't being checked in the same way?

Assuming system includes do need to be differentiated, a solution to the above problem is to have users distinguish system includes on the cppclean call (e.g., using `-I` for system includes and `-i` for non-system includes.). It would then be a simple check in ast.py for whether a given include is in one of those sets of paths. If it is in neither, system could be assumed.

I am happy to submit a pull request if desired.

A practical example can be found from cloning SCRIMMAGE [here](https://github.com/gtri/scrimmage/) and running the following:

```bash
cppclean -I src/proto_conversions/ProtoConversions.cpp
```

issue_title: Unable to patch urllib2 after install_opener has been used.
issue_body:
We're using a custom opener with urllib2 (adding some additional sec features), looks something like so:

```python
urllib2.install_opener(urllib2.build_opener(CustomHTTPSOpener))
```

The opener overwrites the `https_open` method so that a custom connection object can be used. Something like the following:

```python
class CustomHTTPSOpener(urllib2.HTTPSHandler):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        urllib2.HTTPSHandler.__init__(self)
        self._connect_args = kwargs

    def https_open(self, req):
        def connection_wrapper(host, **kwargs):
            all_kwargs = dict(self._connect_args)
            all_kwargs.update(kwargs)
            return CustomHTTPSConnection(host, **all_kwargs)

        return self.do_open(connection_wrapper, req)
```

Then when I use vcrpy, I get the following:

```
TypeError: unbound method __init__() must be called with VCRHTTPConnection myapp/tests/cassette.yaml instance as first argument (got CustomHTTPSConnection instance instead)
```

Any ideas? Thanks

issue_title: Report accurate state of * lock keys
issue_body:
Caps lock, num lock, scroll lock are all toggle modifiers, so if their state is unexpected at the start of the program it'll never be fixed. There should be a way to poll their actual status.

This may be expanded to include other modifiers, so the library starts more ready.

This may require update the functions that handle modifier keys, to take into account 'toggle' keys too.

issue_title: Version information
issue_body:
We currently only store the version in `setup.py`, which is not very useful. There should be a switch `nbstripout --version` and/or `nbstripout version`.

Options for implementing this include:
* [Create a `version.py` module and load in `setup.py` via `execfile`](http://stackoverflow.com/a/2073599) (would require turning `nbstripout` into a package)
* Use [versioneer](https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer)

Any thoughts @mforbes @michaelaye?

issue_title: Use subtypes of AssertionError to support better handling of violations
issue_body:
If each contract (`requires`, `ensures`, etc.) used a distinct subtype of `AssertionError`, it would be possible to programmatically handle various kinds of contract violation.  I imagine something like:

```python
class ContractError(AssertionError):
    """An AssertionError raised by a contract."""
class RequireContractError(ContractError):
    """A ContractError raised because @require(...) was violated."""
# And similarly for Ensure*, Type*, and Invariant*

# Then, to replace `assert foo, "bar" in each place:
if foo:
    raise SpecificContractError("bar")
```

This could be used by a fuzzer - e.g. HypothesisWorks/hypothesis#1474 - to automatically discover valid inputs, by distinguishing between invalid input and other errors.

issue_title: Connection.setencoding() doesn't affect unicode parameter encoding
issue_body:
Reading about [unicode](http://mkleehammer.github.io/pyodbc/unicode.html) my understanding is if I do the following in Python 3 it should encode the parameters as SQL_VARCHAR:

    import pyodbc
    cnxn = pyodbc.connect('DSN=')
    cnxn.setencoding(encoding='utf-8', ctype=pyodbc.SQL_CHAR)
    cursor = cnxn.cursor()
    cursor.execute("SELECT id, name FROM user_table WHERE name = ?", ['scott'])
    print(cursor.fetchone())

However, when I attempt that it is still binding the parameter as `SQL_WVARCHAR` (Python 3.5.2 and pyodbc 4.0.6 on Windows):

		HSTMT               0x000000A0DC7215B0
		UWORD                        1 
		SWORD                        1 <SQL_PARAM_INPUT>

		SQLLEN *            0x000000A0DA403E28

(I have an ODBC driver that doesn't support UTF-16)

Am I misunderstanding how `setencoding()` is supposed to work?

I did a test by copying the 

    info.ParameterType = (enc.ctype == SQL_C_CHAR) ? SQL_VARCHAR : SQL_WVARCHAR;

and similar lines in `param.cpp` from `GetStrInfo()` into `GetUnicodeInfo()` and it seemed to work (see https://github.com/smsearcy/pyodbc/tree/py3-unicode).  However the test was on Fedora and I'm getting a MemoryError with pyodbc 4.0.6 (pyodbc 3.1.1 does not have an issue).  My normal environment is Windows, in which 4.0.6 worked, but I'm not setup to compile packages on Windows so I used Fedora for the test.  I realize this isn't an ideal test scenario but I wanted to get the issue out there while I do a little more digging on the MemoryError.

Here's the trace from the successful test (the MemoryError occurs while loading results):

issue_title: Asynchronous modules should be :D
Support for format_time in tztime module
issue_body:
The tztime module from i3status introduced another ```format_time``` option to enable pango markup: https://i3wm.org/i3status/manpage.html#_tztime

As py3status handles the tztime module, this new option needs to be taken into account when parsing the time's format. Currently, when using ```format_time```, the time stops updating and adds the ```%Y,%m,%d``` formatstrings to the time (see [here](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/__init__.py#L413)).

issue_title: Need a robust connection & connection pool.
issue_body:
happybase currently use Thrift transport's `isOpen()` method (which is in fact the `TSocket` `isOpen()` method)  to determine [whether it should open a new connection or keep the current one](https://github.com/wbolster/happybase/blob/9cbd718c10a3089f234f1eac1236b631e1f8e7cd/happybase/connection.py#L164-L173). 

However, `isOpen()` only tells us whether you have closed this socket explicitly. (See [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L44-L47) and [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L72-L73) in thrift).

So, we should not use `isOpen` to determine whether we need a new connection. but to test the underlying socket object if the it is "dead" and should be closed.

Although I've requested `thrift` to [expose the underlying socket object or to give a method telling if the connection should be closed](https://github.com/apache/thrift/pull/945#issuecomment-196004421). considering it's kind of a "big" project, even if those guys accept my advice, it may take a bit long time to implement the corresponding features. So, Could we fix this issue in `happybase` first? 

I'd like to create a PR if you like :)

issue_title: Allow `python -m honcho` to act the same as `honcho` ?
issue_body:
All is in the title, a lot of python tools (pip, gunicorn, virualenv, venv, ...) are having the same CLI behaviour whether they're called using the setuptools entrypoint, or called with their package's "__main__". The benefit of having both interfaces is that one can run the package even if he does not have his path setup correctly for a given virtualenv. I just need to know the python interpreter path, and can use libraries from there

If we run `python -m honcho` today, it prints: `No module named honcho.__main__; 'honcho' is a package and cannot be directly executed`

What I propose is that `python -m honcho` becomes equivalent to `honcho`, in a given environment. 

If you are ok for this, I can handle implementation, which should be trivial (it's basically only creating a __main__.py file in honcho's package root and calling the same thing as does the setuptools entrypoint there).

Let me know.

issue_title: Playlist sample doesn't enqueue next song
Optional attributes and unknown children count
issue_body:
# What's wrong?
## 1. Optional attributes
If i have a tag which can look like this:
```xml
<sometag>blablabla</sometag>
```
or like this:
```xml
<sometag attr="123">blablabla</sometag>
```
In the first case parse result will look like this:
```python
{
    'sometag': 'blablabla'
}
```
and in the second case it will look like:
```python
{
    'sometag': {
        '@attr': '123',
         '#text': 'blablabla'
    }
}
```
So if I want to get its text content I can't just do something like:
```python
something = parse_result['sometag']['#text']
```
I have to write such ugly things:
```python
something = parse_result['sometag']
something = something['#text'] if type(something) is OrderedDict else something
```




## 2. Unknown children count
If I have a tag which can look like this:
```xml
<parent>
    <child>some text</child>
    <child>other text</child>
<parent>
```
And I don't know exact children count **(child could be only one!)**
So I couldn't iterate over children like this:
```python
for child in parse_result['parent']['child']:
    # some code
```
because list would be used only if there are more than one child.
So in this case I also have to perform some ugly type checking.
Like this:
```python
children = parse_result['parent']['child']
children = children if type(children) is list else [children]
for child in children:
    # some code
```
# Suggestion
I suggest to add special mode (triggered by special optional argument passed to *parse* function).
In this mode it will always use dictionary for describing tags and always use lists for describing children tags.

issue_title: Ambiguous error when loading from yaml example
issue_body:
Hi,

This is probably not a pyswagger issue but I just want to report what I observe and maybe you can decide whether it is or not an issue.

Looking at the 'quick start' section, the sample code loads spec from 

http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.json

My current project prefers YAML format, so naturally I tried letting pyswagger load from this alternative:

http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.yaml

Code:
```py
app = pyswagger.App.load('http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.yaml')
app.prepare()
```

But then I got the following error when preforming `prepare`:
```
TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable
```

The json version can be prepared without problem, so I went on and compared their differences. The key issue was under `paths` section, `responses` part.

```yaml
      responses:
        405:
          description: "Invalid input"
```

```json
    "responses": {
      "405": {
        "description": "Invalid input"
      }
    },
```

In the YAML file the status codes were listed as numbers, but in JSON file they were strings. This will cause error even with `strict` set to `False`.

Adding double quotes in YAML solves the problem, but the file is from the same example source so there could be others like me, confused by this in the future.

issue_title: Anonymous Connection to Public S3 buckets
issue_body:
S3 buckets can be made public, and with boto you can now connect to such buckets without providing an AWS Credentials.

pysparkling currently requires AWS Credentials, when connecting for any connection to S3 to be set as Environment Variables.

To connect to S3 anonymously you can do the following with boto.

boto.connect_s3(anon=True)

It would be great if pysparkling also allowed this when reading files from public S3 buckets.

issue_title: RSCHTMLReader throws bytes/string error
issue_body:
I'm using python 3.5 and trying to process an RSC article (10.1039/C6OB02074G)

I see the error:

`TypeError: %b requires bytes, or an object that implements __bytes__, not 'str'`

The issue seems to be with the **replace_rsc_img_chars** function in rsc.py.

Looking at it the matches that are obtained from parsing the entity xpath (u1 and u2) are unicode strings (see **lines 270, 272**). u1 and u2 are then subsequently used to generate rep (**line 276**) here the code is trying to insert a unicode string into a byte string.

issue_title: range()
issue_body:
Range can have a start, end and step value.
It can be for i in range(5,10):
but it can also be for i in range(10,5,-1):

In that case you would want change the third part of the loop to i += step, instead of i++.

issue_title: Add more flexible support for including/excluding urls
issue_body:
Hi,

I have added some external apps to my projects, and I want to exclude their urls from django-js-reverse. But the problem is that I cannot put namespace for their urls (When I set namespace, app doesn't work).

For example I have installed `django-simple-captcha` and I have added this to my urls.py:

    url(r'^captcha/', include('captcha.urls')),

I want to exclude this app's urls. But when I change this line to:

    url(r'^captcha/', include('captcha.urls', namespace='captcha')),

the captcha app doesn't work.

A whitelist for urls, or an option to exclude urls base on their prefix will help to solve similar issues.

Maybe:

    EXCLUDE_PREFIXES = ['captcha', '...', ...]

But I think a whitelist does the job better:

    INCLUDE_NAMESPACES = ['app1', 'app2', ...]
    INCLUDE_PREFIXES = ['abc', ...]

opencc-python Conversion Does Not Match OpenCC
issue_body:
When running a conversion of "s2twp", the results for opencc-python do not always match those for OpenCC. For example:
OpenCC: "一干 " -> "一干 "
opencc-python: "一干 " -> "一幹 "

Note:
It appears that the opencc-python conversion chain does not honor "group" tag in the configuration file.
The chain is [TWVariantsRevPhrases.txt, TWVariantsRev.txt, TWPhrasesRev.txt, TSPhrases.txt, TSCharacters.txt]
The chain should be [[TWVariantsRevPhrases.txt, TWVariantsRev.txt], TWPhrasesRev.txt, [TSPhrases.txt, TSCharacters.txt]]

I've made changes to example.py and opencc.py appear to fix the problem The implementation is ~6x faster. Because of the large changes, I've decided to just attach the modified files rather than try creating a branch.

[example.py.zip](https://github.com/yichen0831/opencc-python/files/340378/example.py.zip)

[opencc.py.zip](https://github.com/yichen0831/opencc-python/files/340383/opencc.py.zip)

issue_title: listdir() doesn't work at bucket root
issue_body:
`storage.listdir(path)` normalizes `path` as a bucket key, adds a slash, and then passes this prefix to the `boto3` API here:

https://github.com/etianen/django-s3-storage/blob/9d41df75959590ccc50aa499b12e7af0c0fc7d06/django_s3_storage/storage.py#L278

This doesn't seem to work for the bucket root.

Any of `''`, `'/'` or `'.'` gets normalized to `'.'` and passing `Prefix="./"` to `list_objects_v2` doesn't work, as shown below.

```
# expected result with Prefix=""
>>> paginator = self.s3_connection.get_paginator("list_objects_v2")
>>> pages = paginator.paginate(Bucket=self.settings.AWS_S3_BUCKET_NAME, Delimiter="/", Prefix="")

[{'ResponseMetadata': {'RequestId': '8A7908AE11249562', 'HostId': 'YlaPCrxFiS95+BZ1bhFFLv6Efx9iYvfrM4hm/DvSUmLR5TpACQX9tReixvcLP2NnCkw8yM3lU7k=', 'HTTPStatusCode': 200, 'HTTPHeaders': {'x-amz-id-2': 'YlaPCrxFiS95+BZ1bhFFLv6Efx9iYvfrM4hm/DvSUmLR5TpACQX9tReixvcLP2NnCkw8yM3lU7k=', 'x-amz-request-id': '8A7908AE11249562', 'date': 'Sun, 28 May 2017 08:36:33 GMT', 'x-amz-bucket-region': 'eu-west-1', 'content-type': 'application/xml', 'transfer-encoding': 'chunked', 'server': 'AmazonS3'}, 'RetryAttempts': 0}, 'IsTruncated': False, 'Name': 'myks-org--photos', 'Prefix': '.', 'Delimiter': '/', 'MaxKeys': 1000, 'KeyCount': 0}]

[{'ResponseMetadata': {'RequestId': '364AEA1FB40BB0AE', 'HostId': 'j4Ysb++IZlDgXPbrYw7Jq0DjcQAANECKwBXSzUZT3UMzcKGd8phHcyqZd6rMZxX76/AT5HzE/ew=', 'HTTPStatusCode': 200, 'HTTPHeaders': {'x-amz-id-2': 'j4Ysb++IZlDgXPbrYw7Jq0DjcQAANECKwBXSzUZT3UMzcKGd8phHcyqZd6rMZxX76/AT5HzE/ew=', 'x-amz-request-id': '364AEA1FB40BB0AE', 'date': 'Sun, 28 May 2017 08:36:40 GMT', 'x-amz-bucket-region': 'eu-west-1', 'content-type': 'application/xml', 'transfer-encoding': 'chunked', 'server': 'AmazonS3'}, 'RetryAttempts': 0}, 'IsTruncated': False, 'Name': 'myks-org--photos', 'Prefix': '/', 'Delimiter': '/', 'MaxKeys': 1000, 'KeyCount': 0}]

issue_title: Mortality.Org Data
check_low_var does not work if some columns are non-numeric
issue_body:
It appears as though lifelines expects all columns to be numeric.  When fitting a coxPH model, a first step is to call check_low_var. Line 974 in __init__.py
cols = str(list(df.columns[low_var]))

throws an error, since low_var has len = number of numeric columns, whereas df.columns has len = total number of columns. I think this can be fixed by changing line 974 to:
cols = str(list(df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns[low_var]))

issue_title: Accessing tables in a schema doesn't seem to work in legacy DB
issue_body:
I'm not too familiar with MSSQL, so let me know if any terminology is wrong, but is there a supported way to access tables within a schema? For example, say I have a legacy database and there is a schema called `Products`, and that schema contains a table `Coats`.

So I set up my Django model:

```
class Coat(models.Model):
    
    # attributes...

    class Meta:
        is_managed = False
        db_table = 'Coats'  # This is the tricky part
````

There doesn't seem like there is any good way to set the `db_table`. I've tried `Coats`, `Products.Coats`, `COATS`, `Products/Coats`, etc. etc.

However, there is a gross way to make it work that I found [here](http://kozelj.org/django-1-6-mssql-and-schemas/), which uses some injection. So using that, we'd set `db_table = 'Products].[Coats'`. 

Surely someone has faced this before - is there a better way to access tables in a schema? If not, maybe I can submit a PR to institutionalize this method because it was tough to even find a workaround for.

Thanks!

issue_title: Incorrect behavior in aiohttp_apispec_middleware
issue_body:
My problem is if I add `aiohttp_apispec_middleware` in my `MIDDLEWARES` list, exception catching middlewares know nothing about exceptions in apispec
```
MIDDLEWARES = [
    ...,
    exception_middleware,  # Catch exception, format it and respond to client
    SentryMiddleware(settings.SENTRY),  # Send exception data to sentry
    ...,
    aiohttp_apispec_middleware
]
```

In my opinion, the problem is in this piece of code:
```
middlewares.py: 40
except web.HTTPClientError as error:
    return (error_handler or _default_error_handler)(error)
```

If I change it to:
```
except web.HTTPClientError as error:
    raise error
```
everything works correctly

If you agree that this is the problem, I can  make PR with fix.

issue_title: Add no cache headers to response
issue_body:
Django has a decorator called `never_cache` that ensures that requests are, umm, never cached. Caching is complicated, but I think this library should use this functionality to ensure that the maintenance mode is not cached. This way, when maintenance mode is disabled, the site will return quickly.

I just rewrote the middleware for my own project, and [you can see how I implemented this there][1] (it's a small, simple change). 

Thanks for laying this groundwork. Great little middleware.

[1]: https://github.com/freelawproject/courtlistener/blob/master/cl/lib/middleware.py#L51

issue_title: Assign phonenumbers.phonenumber.PhoneNumber type to field raise TypeError
issue_body:
It is not possible to directly assign phonenumbers.phonenumber.PhoneNumber type to field because it raise TypeError: int() argument must be a string or a number, not 'PhoneNumber'.
This error is caused by trying cast phonenumbers.phonenumber.PhoneNumber object to field type object in to_python function.
phonenumbers.phonenumber.PhoneNumber does not support casting, because its constructor has as first argument country_code.
It must be prepared empty field and to this empty value must be merged wanted value by calling merge_from method.

issue_title: Need new publish/unpublish methods (and docs) to match API changes
issue_body:
I was recently bitten by a change to the Shopify API; you cannot hide a published object by changing the "published_at" attribute to None. This affects Product, SmartCollection, CustomCollection, Page, Article, maybe more.

I haven't seen any Shopify announcement, nor any changes to the Ruby API gem. I only found out about it from the follow-up after I called tech support. Looks like the online docs were updated at some point:

https://help.shopify.com/api/reference/smartcollection#update

issue_title: Windows NOT supported for Scala Pacakge
issue_body:
Currently, the build target in Scala package does not contains a dll target to build. Also some tests are not available on windows. In that case Windows build is not available on Scala Package now. I am looking for ways to work this out but I would welcome contributors to collaborate on this. 

Please contact Scala package Maintainer (@nswamy @lanking520 @yzhliu @andrewfayres) if you would like to work on this.

Similar issue:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/8075
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/10549

Let's keep this issue open until we have Windows ready.

Please label this issue as 'Windows', 'Scala'

issue_title: something wrong in batchnorm between ctx.is_train in false and true
issue_body:
I use lstm to train and test, use "cpu only"
I set BatchNormPara with:
eps: 0.001
momentum: 0.9
fix_gamma: false
use_global_state: false

In train phrase, all are good; but in test state, 
when use exec->Forward(false),  I get result with some data -nan(0x400000)
but use exec->Forward(true), all are good;

I check the code, and find that in batch norm phase,  when ctx.is_train == false, it just assign out with "moving_var" and "moving_mean";  
and i check the data in "out" after assign, some part is -nan(0x400000) when the data in "data" and "slope(gamma)" and "bias(beta)" are good.

why this?  why not calc var and mean when ctx.is_train == false?

issue_title: DAG concurrency is not honored
issue_body:
Dear Airflow Maintainers,

Before I tell you about my issue, let me describe my environment:

### Environment

* Version of Airflow: 1.6.2
* Airflow configuration: Running a Scheduler with **LocalExecutor**
* Operating System: 3.13.0-74-generic #118-Ubuntu SMP Thu Dec 17 22:52:10 UTC 2015 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
* Python Version:  2.7.6
* Screen shots of your DAG's status:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4205590/14751524/1f930c86-087f-11e6-824d-2f920f1299e5.png)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4205590/14751546/3222373c-087f-11e6-996e-66ae1ad6ade9.png)


Now that you know a little about me, let me tell you about the issue I am having:

### Description of Issue
In our dag, we set the **dag_args['concurrency'] = 8,** however, when the scheduler starts to run, we can see this concurrency is not being honored, airflow scheduler will run up to num of the 'parallelism' (we set as 25) jobs.

* What did you expect to happen?
dag_args['concurrency'] = 8 is honored, e.g. only run at most 8 jobs concurrently.

* What happened instead?
when the dag starts to run, we can see the concurrency is not being honored, airflow scheduler will run up to the 'parallelism' (we set as 25) jobs.

* Here is how you can reproduce this issue on your machine:
1. **create a dag which contains nothing but 25 parallelized jobs.**
2. set the dag dag_args['concurrency'] = 8
3. set the airflow parallelism to 25
3. then run: airflow scheduler
4. you will see all 25 jobs are scheduled to run, not 8.

issue_title: Enum serializing error - python3.5
issue_body:
jsonpickle encodes list of an objects incorrectly if two neighboring objects contain the same Enum value.

Test code:
```python
import jsonpickle
from enum import Enum


class Type(Enum):
    ONE = 1
    TWO = 2


class SomeObject:
    def __init__(self, num, type):
        self.n = num
        self.t = type

    def __repr__(self):
        return 'SomeObject(n={}, t={})'.format(self.n, self.t)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    a = SomeObject(1, Type.ONE)
    b = SomeObject(2, Type.ONE)
    c = SomeObject(3, Type.TWO)

    # Different enum value
    l1 = [a, c]
    encoded = jsonpickle.encode(l1)
    decoded = jsonpickle.decode(encoded)
    print(decoded)

    # Same enum value
    l2 = [a, b]
    encoded = jsonpickle.encode(l2)  # error
    decoded = jsonpickle.decode(encoded)
    print(decoded)
```

issue_title: boto3.s3.transfer set Metadata incorrectly
issue_body:
http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/boto3/s3/transfer.html says:
# Setting metadata
transfer.upload_file('/tmp/myfile', 'bucket', 'key',
                 extra_args={'Metadata': {'a': 'b', 'c': 'd'}})

If I upload a file, e.g. index.html with the following command:

```
from boto3.s3.transfer import S3Transfer
awsclient = boto3.client('s3', 'eu-west-1')
transfer = S3Transfer(awsclient)

transfer.upload_file("index.html",OUTBUCKET,"index.html",extra_args={'Metadata': {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}})
```

It sets up the S3 object metadata to:
**x-amz-meta-content-type: text/html**

Instead of the expected:
**Content-Type: text/html**

The main problem e.g.:  uploading the index.html file with boto3 in a S3 static website hosting, because of the WEB browsers downloads it instead opens.

issue_title: Wheel support
issue_body:
http://pythonwheels.com/

Right now, only the `tar.gz` file is being distributed on PyPI for [icalendar](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/icalendar), which isn't _that much_ of an issue, but it does add some time to installing the package.

This package should be compatible with the Wheel format, considering it doesn't appear to have any C dependencies and it is compatible with both Python 2 and 3. As a result, you should only need to generate a universal wheel and then everyone (on all systems) will get the ability to install icalendar with just the wheel, without having to do any extra work.

ciso8601.parse_datetime('20140203 04:05:.123456')

Actual:

datetime.datetime(2014, 2, 3, 4, 5, 0, 123456)

issue_title: Existing virtualenvs ignored on Windows when using cmd.exe
issue_body:
This is an issue caused by some odd quirk in how cmd.exe handles path names on Windows.

As a result, it is possible to end up in a situation, where running a pipenv command (such as pipenv shell) in a directory, for which a valid virtualenv already exists, causes pipenv to ignore it and create a brand new one, with the same name (except for the hash).

I have recently run into this issue at work. I have a workstation running Windows 10 and Python 2.7, which usually gets called from cmd.exe. What I have just discovered, is that depending on what command I use when changing directories, a slightly different path gets reported as pipfile_location in project.py:

> cd c:\tmp\test

results in "c:\tmp\test" getting set as the current directory in cmd.exe and "c:\tmp\test\Pipfile" getting returned from pipfile_location

vs.

> cd C:\tmp\test

which leads to "C:\tmp\test" getting set as the current directory in cmd.exe and "C:\tmp\test\Pipfile" being the reported location of the Pipfile.

Note the lower- vs. upper-case drive letter, which results in a completely different hash value for the pipfile location, and in consequence, a completely different name being returned from virtualenv_name in project.py, which then triggers the creation of a brand new virtualenv. 
This seems to affect only drive letters -- directory names are fine ("cd C:\TMP" produces "C:\tmp" on my machine). PowerShell does not seem to suffer from this.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type : Windows 10 x64 (build 1709)
1. Python version: 2.7.14
1. Pipenv version: 9.0.1

##### Expected result

'pipenv shell' activates the existing environment

##### Actual result

'pipenv shell' creates a new environment alongside the existing one

##### Steps to replicate

Provide the steps to replicate (which usually at least includes the commands and the Pipfile).

With Python 2.7 (and pipenv) installed and added to the PATH env variable:

1. Start the command prompt (Win+R, cmd.exe)
2. From within the command prompt, create a directory somewhere on a disk (e.g. C:\temp\test)
3. Go to that directory, typing the drive letter in upper case (cd C:\temp\test)
4. Call pipenv to create a new virtualenv for that directory (e.g. pipenv install pylint)
5. Change the current working directory to some other directory (e.g. cd C:\)
6. Go back to the test directory, typing the drive letter in lower case (cd c:\temp\test)
7. Call pipenv shell (or pipenv --venv) inside the test directory -- pipenv will
create a new virtualenv, ignoring the one created in step 4

issue_title: Pipfile python_version not taken into account when generating lockfile
issue_body:

Pipenv generates a lockfile with installable packages and then installs them.

pyyaml = "*"

[dev-packages]

rope = "*"
ipython = "*"
mock = "*"
pylint = "*"
pylint-quotes = "*"
yapf = "*"

With this Pipfile both pylint-quotes and ipython will fail since pylint-quotes 1.9 and ipython 6.3.0 are both incompatible with python 2.7.14

issue_title: Support repairing cffi PyPy extensions
issue_body: CFFI extensions compiled for PyPy do not export ``'init' + modname`` nor ``'PyInit_' + modname`` symbols but instead they export ``'_cffi_pypyinit_' + modname`` and thus they are skipped by ``auditwheel``. However actually ``auditwheel`` can repair them. As an example one may take a look at ``python-snappy`` - it uses cffi for PyPy. And proof for using ``_cffi_pypyinit_`` prefix may be found at ``cffi/recompiler.py``.

issue_title: Implement "bootstrap-buildout.py" behavior to always download setuptools and only use a local file if set by a parameter
issue_body:
The primary mode of installation of setuptools is to [use ez_setup.py](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools#unix-wget): but if a beginner downloads a corrupted zip for some reason (check https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/557 for example) and don't delete the corrupted setuptools zip before running ez_setup.py again, the installation can't continue.

Since this is confusing to beginners the idea is to have a similar behavior like bootstrap-buildout.py: always download setuptools, but if you need for some reason to use a local file, use a parameter to have it ([--setuptools-to-dir](https://github.com/buildout/buildout/blob/9ba1e3170a474e6139c9a7eadd32a498c22cb3bb/bootstrap/bootstrap.py#L70) comes to mind).

ez_setup.py has now [a specific branch](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/tree/bootstrap) and a test suite and this improvement should be done there.

issue_title: Merge setup.cfg spec with pyproject.toml one and deprecate setup.py and setup.cfg
issue_body:
Now we have a bunch of files. It's a mess:
1 python packages metadata is in fact generated by ```setup.py``` script. This means if you need to parse it you'll have to execute the script. 
2 Recently ```pyproject.toml``` was introduced, which contains for now only the description of the packages needed to build a package. Why do we need this? It can be put into setup.cfg

So I propose to
0 ship setuptools with python
1 merge setup.cfg into pyproject.toml and publish a PEP
2 setuptools should be workable without setup.py, only with pyproject.toml in a repo
3 pyproject.toml should allow additional fields to support non-standard extensions
4 pyproject.toml is the primary source of metainfo. setup.py should be executed if and only if it pyproject.toml uses some variables from it.
5 Write a warning in red that setup.py is deprecated each time ```setuptools.setup``` is used

issue_title: "plating" decorator for quickly constructing simple sites
issue_body:
Right now it's a bit tedious to construct a Klein site with the usual accoutrements of a simple website; some consistent navigation and page chrome, different things in the content area of each page, et cetera.

I propose that we add a decorator that assists in the construction of such sites, making it easy to quickly type some templates in-line, then gradually move them to external XML templates as they become more complex.

The decorator I propose decorates functions which return dictionaries of slot-filling data, so the decoratee's job is simply to populate the correct model data and the *decorator's* job is to fill out the `Element` with the appropriate twisted.web.template stuff to make it render HTML.

This is related to #78, in that ideally, the model data returned by the function could be returned as serialized JSON if the client asks for JSON, and only converted into HTML with styling if the client is a browser asking for HTML.

Prototype will be attached below.

issue_title: Different results in source detection with DAOStarFinder versus DAOPhotPSFPhotometry
issue_body:
As far as I understand, the `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` class uses the `DAOStarFinder` class to search for sources.

I'm seeing rather different results using both classes on the same image:

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6054951/20117495/9ebf8d24-a5df-11e6-927c-50981392bdd4.png)

Shouldn't both return the same results if I use the same parameters?

The code is here, if needed I can upload the original .fits file.

```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

from astropy.io import fits
from astropy.stats import gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm

from photutils.utils import cutout_footprint
from photutils.background import MADStdBackgroundRMS
from photutils.psf import IntegratedGaussianPRF
from photutils.psf import DAOPhotPSFPhotometry
from photutils import DAOStarFinder
from photutils import CircularAperture

# Load data.
image_file = 'test.fits'

# Image data.
hdulist = fits.open(image_file)
hdu_data = hdulist[0].data
hdulist.close()
# Crop image
crop = cutout_footprint(hdu_data, (2100, 1800), (500, 1100))
hdu_crop = crop[0]

bkgrms = MADStdBackgroundRMS()
std = bkgrms(hdu_crop)
thresh = 50. * std
sigma_psf = 5.
fwhm_sigma = sigma_psf * gaussian_sigma_to_fwhm
fitshape = int(3 * np.ceil(fwhm_sigma) // 2 * 2 + 1)

# DAOStarFinder
stfind = DAOStarFinder(threshold=thresh, fwhm=fwhm_sigma)
sources = stfind(hdu_crop)
print(sources)

# DAOPhotPSFPhotometry
psf_model = IntegratedGaussianPRF(sigma=sigma_psf)
photometry = DAOPhotPSFPhotometry(
    crit_separation=3. * fwhm_sigma, threshold=thresh, fwhm=fwhm_sigma,
    psf_model=psf_model, fitshape=fitshape)
result_tab = photometry(image=hdu_crop)
print(result_tab)

median, std = np.median(hdu_crop), np.std(hdu_crop)

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.title('DAOStarFinder')
plt.imshow(hdu_crop, cmap='viridis', aspect=1, interpolation='nearest',
           origin='lower', vmin=0., vmax=median + std)
positions = (sources['xcentroid'], sources['ycentroid'])
apertures = CircularAperture(positions, r=4.)
apertures.plot(color='red', lw=1.5)
plt.colorbar(orientation='horizontal', fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.title('DAOPhotPSFPhotometry')
plt.imshow(hdu_crop, cmap='viridis', aspect=1, interpolation='nearest',
           origin='lower', vmin=0., vmax=median + std)
positions = (result_tab['x_fit'], result_tab['y_fit'])
apertures = CircularAperture(positions, r=4.)
apertures.plot(color='red', lw=1.5)
plt.colorbar(orientation='horizontal', fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

plt.show()
```

issue_title: nupic.framworks.opf.opf_utils.PredictionElement is never used
issue_body:
As [described here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/17969b843e944c3a67fbb4cd7e049a6af585c91b/src/nupic/frameworks/opf/opf_utils.py#L271-L290):

```python
# PredictionElement- represents a predicted record and its asssociated
#                     bit-string encoding for a network's sensor region and/or
#                     the classification of that input as produced by
#                     a classifier.
#
# predictionRow:  A sequence of field values where each element is the predicted
#                 value in the format specified by getDecodedFieldMetaInfo().
#                 This is the output of topDownCompute() for each encoder
#
# predictionEncodings: A sequence of numpy arrays, where each element is the
#                      binary representation of the corresponding predicted field
#                      in "predictionRow".
#
# classification: The classification category of this input.
#

PredictionElement = namedtuple("PredictionElement",
                                ("predictionRow",
                                 "predictionEncodings",
                                 "classification"))
```

This named tuple is seemingly never used in our codebase. The only place I found even a vague reference was in [`tests/integration/nupic/opf/expgenerator_test.py`](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/17969b843e944c3a67fbb4cd7e049a6af585c91b/tests/integration/nupic/opf/expgenerator_test.py#L406-L461), but the `test_PredictionElement` test does not use the named tuple, nor does any other code.

issue_title: AnomalyLikelihood with default timestamp behavior
issue_body:
When time stamp is not specified to `AnomalyLikelihood.anomalyProbability()` it defaults to `None` which results in using current time! `datettime.now()`. 

That was done for convenience when you can't/don't want to pass the `timestamp` manually. On the other hand, passing `None`, one would expect it is ignored - which is not. As seen in : 
https://github.com/breznak/nupic/commit/0fc0acfdc8ae977f9d6f9f995cd046ead04021eb

Possible solution: 
- make 2 "default" choices, eg 
  * `"now"` uses current time (as used now) 
  * `None` would use iterations instead of times, so 
```
l1 = AnomalyLikelihood()
l2 = AnomalyLikelihood()

l1.anomalyProbability("something", 0.45)
l2.anomalyProbability("something", 0.45)

l1 == l2 # True
```

issue_title: BTrees not finding contained keys on parallel access with gevent
issue_body:
This grows out of the discussion here https://github.com/zopefoundation/BTrees/issues/21 What happened in my program is, I have some greenlets writing changes to a `BTree`, and another greenlet committing the changes. Doing this with enough load, and the `BTree`s start to throw `KeyError`s when reading keys, they're supposed to contain. This only happens when using `ClientStorage` and a server, not on direct access to a `DemoStorage`. So please let me know if I should redirect this error report to the ZEO package.

I'm aware it's not an ideal setup, and I probably should have used a separate transaction manager for every single greenlet. Though, I was using this setup before on direct file access, and thought having the database run as a separate server process should be a drop-in replacement. Here the code:

```python
"""
As counterpart for this test, run a ZEO server with this config:

<zeo>
   address /tmp/zodbsock
</zeo>

<demostorage>
</demostorage>

"""

from gevent import monkey, joinall, spawn, coros, sleep
monkey.patch_all()

import logging
import random
import string

import transaction
from BTrees.OOBTree import OOBTree
from ZODB import DB, DemoStorage
from ZEO import ClientStorage


class Database(object):
    _closed = True

    def __init__(self, socket):
        self.socket = socket
        self.commit_lock = coros.RLock()
        self.open()

    def open(self):
        if not self._closed:
            return

        if self.socket:
            self.storage = ClientStorage.ClientStorage(self.socket)
        else:
            self.storage = DemoStorage.DemoStorage()

        self.db = DB(self.storage)
        self.tm = transaction.TransactionManager()
        self.connection = self.db.open(transaction_manager=self.tm)
        self.root = self.connection.root()
        self._closed = False

    def close(self):
        if self._closed:
            return
        self.tm.commit()
        self.connection.close()
        self.db.close()
        self.storage.close()
        del self.root
        del self.connection
        del self.db
        del self.storage
        self._closed = True

    def commit(self):
        with self.commit_lock:
            self.tm.commit()
            self.tm.begin()
            print("Commited")


NOBJS = 10000


def randstr(n):
    return ''.join(random.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(n))


def keyfunc(k1, k2='asdf', **kwargs):
    return str(k1) + '_' + k2


def init(sock=None):
    db = Database(sock)
    if 'test' in db.root:
        del db.root['test']
        db.commit()
    db.root['test'] = test = OOBTree()

    for i in xrange(NOBJS):
        test[keyfunc(i)] = randstr(20)
    db.commit()
    return db


def _stress_inner(tree, j):
    """ Read, updated and insert data """
    i = random.randint(0, NOBJS - 1)
    # Without reading, no errors happen
    _ = tree[keyfunc(i)]
    tree[keyfunc(i)] = randstr(30)
    # Adding no items reduces the KeyErrors
    tree[keyfunc(i + j * 1000, 'extra')] = randstr(20)


def stress(db, i):
    tree = db.root['test']
    for j in range(100):
        _stress_inner(tree, j)
        # Using a plain sleep() reduces the chance for KeyErrors
        sleep(random.random() / 1000)


def main():
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
    # Using plain init() and the KeyErrors are gone
    db = init('/tmp/zodbsock')
    try:
        stressers = [spawn(stress, db, i) for i in xrange(100)]
        while not all(s.ready() for s in stressers):
            db.commit()
            sleep(0.1)
        joinall(stressers)
        db.commit()
    finally:
        db.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

The errors I get, look like this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):

    result = self._run(*self.args, **self.kwargs)
  File "crashdemo.py", line 106, in stress
    _stress_inner(tree, j)
  File "crashdemo.py", line 97, in _stress_inner
    _ = tree[keyfunc(i)]
KeyError: '2063_asdf'
<Greenlet at 0x7fdcdff6e7d0: stress(<__main__.Database object at 0x7fdce040b190>, 36)> failed with KeyError
```

Depending a bit on luck, I'd say they happen in 90% of the cases, maybe 50 per run. As you can see from the code, all these missing keys got created beforehand, so they should be available.

issue_title: Extract Products/SiteAccess?
issue_body:
The `VirtualHostMonster` seems to be perfectly usable with a WSGI setup. (We use it in Zope 2.13 with the WSGI publisher.)

I think it should either be moved back to the `Zope` package or into a separate package `Products.SiteAccess`.

Or is there another story of virtual hosting I am not aware of?

issue_title: Feature Request: watch class constructor methods on android
issue_body:
Would it be possible to add watches for class constructor methods on android?

From inspecting the code, it does not appear this is currently possible.

issue_title: user fields-need to store more than just required fields
issue_body:
Djoser does not allow saving extra user fields like first name, last name UNLESS they are REQUIRED.

Can we have it save additional fields if I am using a custom user model?

issue_title: Framework does not get HeartBeats
issue_body:
Hi @windreamer,
In this commit, https://github.com/douban/pymesos/commit/04501d10f8568065f626ec3fe8689d4f8fdbf9ee#diff-ab924b88fb1411cfeab6024b822161a8R95 , a change was made to suppress the Heart Beats events to the framework. Was there a reason behind it?

issue_title: What is the recommended way for writing tests ?
issue_body:
I'm on writing some tests regarding authentication and I wonder how should I write them the "standard way".

Following the [DRF](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/testing/#apiclient) tutorial didn't help me a lot.

Maybe it's related with #174 

Thanks

issue_title: lxml is needed in new toolchain
issue_body:
Ran into https://github.com/kivy/buildozer/issues/451 recently. Previously on the old toolchain this functionality for my app was working, and as I had fallback imports as lxml documentation advises, I didn't notice it had stopped working. However I did add more xml related features on desktop and this caused the android version to break silently.

Basically lxml has etree.so and objectify.so which are binary (cython), and need to be recompiled for use in android. I would append this to https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/issues/464 but it was closed as being too generic (I think).

issue_body:
Hi all, I am using Python 3 on Mac OS X. I would like to download S&P 500 data using yahoo finance (somehow it is still working but then throwing an error) and using google finance.

The code I am using 
`from datetime import *`

`import pandas_datareader.data as data`
`import pickle`
`import requests`

`SnP = pd.DataFrame()`
`start = dt.datetime(2000, 1, 1)`
`end = datetime.now()`
`for ticker in tickers:`
          `---stock = data.DataReader(ticker, data_source, start, end)`

Given that: 
- tickers is a list of stock tickers, i.e `tickers = ['MMM', 'ABT', 'ABBV', 'ACN',...,'ZION', 'ZTS']` 
- data_source is either `'yahoo'` or `'google'`

1. Using yahoo API, i.e `data_source = 'yahoo'` the code works for the first few tickers then throws me the following error:
`raise RemoteDataError('Unable to read URL: {0}'.format(url))
pandas_datareader._utils.RemoteDataError: Unable to read URL: https://query1.finance.yahoo.com/v7/finance/download/AYI?period1=946681200&period2=1512946799&interval=1d&events=history&crumb=hnxNmU%5Cu002FEQJS`
However, if only used with one ticker, the same code works perfectly fine, i.e
`import pandas_datareader.data as data`
`start = datetime(2016, 12, 31)`
`end = datetime.now()`
`AAPL = data.DataReader('APPL', 'yahoo', start, end)`

2. When I use google API, i.e `data_source = 'google'`, I am getting this error:
`return b.decode(encoding or 'utf-8')
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xfc in position 18101: invalid start byte`

Would you please help me out with error 1 and 2? I already googled for those errors, and saw related topics on github and ..., withtout success in making the code work.

ps: I am new to Python. Using it since a week. 

Thank you :) Ines

issue_title: Provide a log directory for each test
issue_body:
When there are multiple tests in a class, it is very helpful to group output files per each test for debugging purposes.

To achieve this, Mobly should provide such directory for each test. This way, the test could dump debug info to this directory in `on_fail`.

We choose this design because it maintains the separation of controller and framework responsibilities. The controller module does not need to be aware of the execution phase of a test, e.g. `AndroidDevice` does not need to worry about dumping logs at a particular point in test execution.

And the Mobly framework does not need to know how to get a particular type of log for a particular test. A test class could decide at which point of execution to dump which type of log for a test.

issue_title: secondary install breaks subsequent normal installs
issue_body:
It has taken me a while to fully quantify this problem, but it seems that these steps to reproduce will suffice:

 - switch to the v1 branch
 - (not sure if necessary) install normally with `make install`
 - install secondary install with `make v1-refresh`
 - switch to master branch
 - `make clean` and `make install`

Now check with `tower-cli --version` which version is in use. Of course, 3.2.0 is expected, but 3.1.8 is returned.

Here is a gist from the install when I do it:

https://gist.github.com/AlanCoding/5fd59ec9a5e822d50878184a5cdc3c67

The entry script for `tower-cli` _does_ reference the right version.

```
cat $(which tower-cli)
#!/Users/alancoding/.virtualenvs/cli/bin/python
# EASY-INSTALL-ENTRY-SCRIPT: 'ansible-tower-cli==3.2.0','console_scripts','tower-cli'
__requires__ = 'ansible-tower-cli==3.2.0'
import re
import sys
from pkg_resources import load_entry_point

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(
        load_entry_point('ansible-tower-cli==3.2.0', 'console_scripts', 'tower-cli')()
    )

```

Inexplicably, this entry script lands on the 3.1.8 version.

I'm looking into what is going on, but unclear what root cause is at the moment.

issue_title: Support for uint64
issue_body:
Awesome package! 

Pyrobuf seems to have an issue with serializing (or deserializing, not sure which) u64int that is greater than 0x7FFFFFFF (31 bits) as seen in the following example.  the Json and Dict representations seem to be fine.

System: Mac OSX using 64 bit python and Cython from an integrated Anaconda install:


Example:
Using pyrobuf-0.0.2a4 with the following protobuf definition:

message A {
        optional  uint64 a0 = 1;
}

Iand the following code:
pb2 = a_proto.A()
pb2.a0 = 0x7FFFFFFF
print pb2.a0
print pb2.SerializeToJson()
print pb2.SerializeToDict()
print a_proto.A().FromString(pb2.SerializeToString())

print
pb = a_proto.A()
pb.a0 = 0x80000000
print pb.a0
print pb.SerializeToJson()
print pb.SerializeToDict()
print a_proto.A().FromString(pb.SerializeToString())

Produces the following result:
------------------------
2147483647
{"a0": 2147483647}
{'a0': 2147483647L}
a0: 2147483647

2147483648

issue_title: Invalid JSON RPC 2.0 error responses
issue_body:
Some errors cause response objects that violate the JSON RPC 2.0 spec, because they have both, a "result" and "error" member set. An example would be MethodNotFoundError.

See https://github.com/cenobites/flask-jsonrpc/blob/723db17b1ef11d15d4180fd0e372b9a51723f4e4/flask_jsonrpc/site.py#L186-195 .
It seems that the version is initialized to late in the marked block and early exceptions are handled with the JSON RPC 1.0 spec, because the version_hint is always "1.0".

issue_title: saved parameters values don't match
issue_body:
I posted a question here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/dynet-users/uaJajWicH9g 

It's about this weird issue I'm having with my saved parameter values not matching those that I see when I call `dy.parameter(parameter_X).npvalue().

What's even weirder is when I built a simple, dummy example to accompany this issue, the problem did *not* occur.

Anyway, here's the toy example I built:

```
import dynet_config
import dynet as dy
import numpy as np

model = dy.ParameterCollection()

builders = [
    dy.GRUBuilder(1, 10, 7, model),
    dy.GRUBuilder(1, 10, 7, model)
]

pV = model.add_parameters((14, 2))
pb = model.add_parameters(2)

E = model.add_lookup_parameters((12, 8))
ET = model.add_lookup_parameters((7, 2))

trainer = dy.AdamTrainer(model)

dy.renew_cg()
# value of V before any training
V_original = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

# save without training
dy.save(
	"/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_without_training",
	[E, ET] + builders + [pV, pb]
)

# load and compare
loaded_model_without_training = dy.ParameterCollection()
loaded_params_without_training = dy.load(
	"/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_without_training",
	loaded_model_without_training
)

V_loaded_without_training = dy.parameter(loaded_params_without_training[4]).npvalue()

# compare V_original and V_loaded
print("V_original == V_loaded_without_training?")
print(np.all(V_original == V_loaded_without_training))

# train
for i in range(1000):
    dy.renew_cg()
    for datapoint, tags, labels in [
    ([1,2,3], [1, 1, 1], [0,0,1]),
    ([0,3,4], [2, 2, 2], [0,0,1])
    ]:
        V = dy.parameter(pV)
        b = dy.parameter(pb)
        words_in = [E[wid] for wid in datapoint]
        tags_in = [ET[tid] for tid in tags]
        init_states = builders[0].initial_state()
        init_states_b = builders[1].initial_state()
        in_ = [dy.concatenate([w, t]) for w,t in zip(words_in, tags_in)]
        in_b = [dy.concatenate([ww, tt]) for ww,tt in zip(reversed(words_in), reversed(tags_in))]
        hidden = [x.output() for x in init_states.add_inputs(in_)]
        hidden_b = [x.output() for x in init_states_b.add_inputs(in_b)]
        hidden_c = [dy.concatenate([h, hb]) for h, hb in zip(hidden, hidden_b)]
        outs = [dy.transpose(dy.transpose(hc) * V) + b for hc in hidden_c]
        losses = [dy.pickneglogsoftmax(o,l) for o,l in zip(outs, labels)]
        losses_summed = dy.esum(losses)
        losses_summed.backward()
        trainer.update()

dy.renew_cg()
V_after_training = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

print("testing")
dy.renew_cg()
for datapoint, tags, labels in [
    ([1,2,3], [1, 1, 1], [0,0,1]),
    ([0,3,4], [2, 2, 2], [0,0,1])
    ]:
    V = dy.parameter(pV)
    b = dy.parameter(pb)
    words_in = [E[wid] for wid in datapoint]
    tags_in = [ET[tid] for tid in tags]
    init_states = builders[0].initial_state()
    init_states_b = builders[1].initial_state()
    in_ = [dy.concatenate([w, t]) for w,t in zip(words_in, tags_in)]
    in_b = [dy.concatenate([ww, tt]) for ww,tt in zip(reversed(words_in), reversed(tags_in))]
    hidden = [x.output() for x in init_states.add_inputs(in_)]
    hidden_b = [x.output() for x in init_states_b.add_inputs(in_b)]
    hidden_c = [dy.concatenate([h, hb]) for h, hb in zip(hidden, hidden_b)]
    outs = [dy.transpose(dy.transpose(hc) * V) + b for hc in hidden_c]
    print(outs)


dy.renew_cg()
V_after_testing = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

dy.save(
    "/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_after_training",
	[E, ET] + builders + [pV, pb]
)

loaded_model_after_training = dy.ParameterCollection()

loaded_params_after_training = dy.load(
    "/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_after_training",
    loaded_model_after_training
)

# load V
dy.renew_cg()
V_loaded_after_training = dy.parameter(loaded_params_after_training[4]).npvalue()

print("V_loaded")
print(V_loaded_after_training)
print("V_after_testing")
print(V_after_testing)
print("V_after_training")
print(V_after_training)
```

But as I said, in this case the parameter values for `V` when loaded *do exactly* match those I see from `dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()`.  

My script that is having the problem is incredibly long and hard to follow, and so I do not want to subject you all to that.  But Graham suggested opening an issue nonetheless.

Thanks in advance for any advice, and for continuing to develop this great package.
-M

issue_title: Better Support for "in-place" Operations
issue_body:
Many operations such as `reshape`, `no_backprop`, etc. don't need to make a memory copy in the forward pass. However, these nodes still result in doing memory allocation and copying memory (before autobatching they didn't result in a memory copy, but the autobatching functionality required them to move memory, so now everything moves memory).

It would be nice to have a way to note in-place operators and prevent them from using extra memory, etc. The one thing we need to be careful about is that they don't break autobatching.

issue_title: Bigtable `PartialRowsData` is not an iterable.
issue_body:
It appears to me that if I want to scan through all rows of a Bigtable, [`Table.read_rows()`](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/bigtable-table.html#gcloud.bigtable.table.Table.read_rows) is the function to use. This returns a [PartialRowsData](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/bigtable-row-data.html#gcloud.bigtable.row_data.PartialRowsData).

Why is `PartialRowsData` not an iterable? I'd like to consume all the rows for a "hello bigtable" sample by iterating through them. Something like

```
print('Scan for all greetings:')
for row in table.read_rows():
    print(row.cells[COLUMN_FAMILY_NAME][COLUMN_NAME.encode('UTF-8')][0].decode('UTF-8'))
```

Instead, it appears that I need to use a while loop and watch a dictionary that gets populated. I think this means that even if I don't need to store all the rows in memory at once, they still do. That's both inefficient and not following Python idioms (of generators / iterables).

issue_title: Should we provide batching across services?
issue_body:
This was brought up by @squee1945 in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/issues/557#issuecomment-90101833

> e.g., I can't batch a storage request together with a datastore request (please correct me if I'm wrong).

If we wanted to batch these together, the API might be pretty tricky, but could look like...

```python
with gcloud.Batch() as batch:
  batch.datastore.put(...)
  batch.storage.upload_file(...)
```

I'm not sure how much value this adds, but wanted to discuss it in a separate issue so that we can make a decision...

My vote is: :-1: -- I don't think the value offsets the cost.

issue_title: Pants expects a 'pants.ini' file even when passed a PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE
issue_body:
EDIT: 

The original issue is below, John clarified to me that this is indeed the intended behavior. I am reframing this as a documentation request. The [doc page](https://pantsbuild.github.io/setup_repo.html) that deals with pants.ini says that you _should_ have a file called pants.ini and is regularly says things to the effect of "In your config file (usually pants.ini),..."

In actuality, 
1) There __must__ be a file called exactly 'pants.ini' and 
2) It will be interpolated at the beginning of every run, no matter whether the user sets PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE or not. 

I knew that there had to be an ini file, but the specific requirements above might be a nice addition to the doc page.

ORIGINAL ISSUE

To reproduce this issue, 
```
mateo:pants mateo$ cat pants-other.ini
[DEFAULT]
pants_version: 0.0.57
mateo:pants mateo$ PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE="['pants-other.ini']" ./pants --version
0.0.57
mateo:pants mateo$ mv pants.ini pantss.ini
mateo:pants mateo$ PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE="['pants-other.ini']" ./pants --version
/Users/mateo/dev/pants/src/python/pants/base/build_environment.py:36: DeprecationWarning: BaseException.message has been deprecated as of Python 2.6
  print(e.message, file=sys.stderr)
Could not find pants.ini!
mateo:pants mateo$
```

I came across this because I am now convinced that pants is still interpolating anything it find in pants.ini for every single run, even if the PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE is set, e.g. the following two commands are equivalent:
```
PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE="['pants-other.ini']" ./pants --version
PANTS_CONFIG_OVERRIDE="['pants.ini', 'pants-jenkins.ini']" ./pants --version
```

This may be by design, it does say `OVERRIDE`. But I contend that if I pass a program a different config file than the default, I no longer expect the default settings to be applied. If you disagree, feel free to mark wontfix or close, I have already worked around this internally.

issue_title: Switch local process execution to use spawn_async not output_async
issue_body:
Here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/26f1d4284d026c633653553bb36681b5cc8be7b5/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/local.rs#L123 we execute a process, and only get its output when it finishes.

In order to support streaming of output, and in order to get partial output on timing out, it would be useful for us to call `spawn_async` instead of `output_async`, and to accumulate the process output ourselves, rather than relying on `output_async` to accumulate the output for us where we can't see it.

This will require configuring output streams on the `Command`, and changing the structure of how we consume the futures a little.

issue_title: Float literals tripping up evaluator?
issue_body:
Hi there, this may just be a case of something caused by our internal setup, but I have a pmml of an xgboost model generated by sklearn2pmml 0.36.0.  The response variable is float and binary, and i with what I assume is a somewhat recent change, the regression tables in the regression model section name the targetCategory with `.0` in the literals:

```
					<RegressionTable intercept="0.0" targetCategory="1.0">
						<NumericPredictor name="xgbValue" coefficient="1.0"/>
					</RegressionTable>
					<RegressionTable intercept="0.0" targetCategory="0.0"/>
				</RegressionModel>
```
Our evaluation system uses jpmml-evaluator 1.3.1 and with a pmml specified like the above, the evaluated output is all zeros. If the literals don't have decimal places, it is happy and produces nonzero outputs with the expected values:
```
					<RegressionTable intercept="0.0" targetCategory="1">
						<NumericPredictor name="xgbValue" coefficient="1.0"/>
					</RegressionTable>
					<RegressionTable intercept="0.0" targetCategory="0"/>
				</RegressionModel>
```
I realize we are using an older pmml-evaluator version but the evaluation team is cagey about upgrading.  Is there an easy way to address this, like by displaying literals without decimal places when possible?  Casting the response to integer before training resolves the issue also but we would need to start doing that across a lot of models. Thanks for any insight you can offer.

issue_title: Adding Normalization to TfidfVectorizer
issue_body:
Hi Villu,

Following issue #68 , we have been attempting to add support for normalized tfidfVectorizer, since our tfidfVectorizer was part of the pipeline. The way I went about doing this is rather hacky but is:
I created a python class called NormalizedFeatureExtraction and in that class, I copied the logistics of scikit learn's tfidfVectorizer but I enforced normalization by removing the norm parameter from the __init__ method and also removing self.norm and instead, normalizing the vectors wherever needed. However, to satisfy the need of the java side, I still return None for 
```
    @property
    def norm(self):
        return None
```
and I kept your implementation of jpmml tfidfVectorizer as its corresponding java side.
I wanted to ask you if there is any better way to do this? And also, when making this change to the python side, do I have to make any changes to the java side as well? Is there any reason why the jpmml tfidfVectorizer throws an exception when norm is not None? Could having normalization the way I have added it, without any change to the java side, potentially cause any logistic problems?
Many thanks for your clarifications.

issue_title: ui-menu keyboard navigation
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Navigation between `menuitems` seems to be working only with `Tab/Shift+Tab` keys.

### Expected behavior

According to [WAI-ARIA practices](https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices/#menu), the navigation between `menuitems` should be possible with `Up/Down` arrow keys as well as with `Tab/Shift+Tab` keys. The latter is actually not recommended in case of the complex navigational structures and menus with deep nested levels, but since we have 3 lonely links inside this menu, it's not such a big faux-pas, yet...

### User-facing consequences

When using keyboard to navigate, user expects to be able to navigate among menu options with the arrow keys.

### Context

* Kolibri version: PEX build from #2623 
* Operating system: Ubuntu 16.10
* Browser: Firefox & Chrome

issue_title: os_release incorrectly detects openstack version by keystone-middleware package if openstack-origin is not present
issue_body:
For most of the OpenStack charms openstack-origin is present, however, subordinates may not always have this option because they rely on a primary charm's versions (e.g. keystone-ldap charm) given that they share a machine and debian packages (not sure how snaps will be handled in this case).

https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L514-L535

charms.openstack has default_select_release which uses python-keystonemiddleware as a package to be passed to os_release unconditionally:
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L532
https://github.com/openstack/charms.openstack/blob/389d928ba50915560b2140e022a663035fa86a4b/charms_openstack/charm/defaults.py#L82-L101

There is no definition for python-keystonemiddleware in PACKAGE_CODENAMES
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L197

which is why OPENSTACK_CODENAMES are used:

https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L466-L480
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L147-L162

For python-keystonemiddleware this results in KeyError because it uses different versions from the ones in OPENSTACK_CODENAMES (<year>.<month> versioning has no relevance):
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L476-L480
A sample unfiltered trace (search for "KeyError: '4.14'"):
https://gist.github.com/dshcherb/4789ea8c16db45617b68bdd6bb59e656

And, finally, _os_rel == base == 'essex' is returned from os_release:
https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/v0.18.3/charmhelpers/contrib/openstack/utils.py#L534

This is quite problematic as charms.openstack doesn't take release_pkg into account even if it is present in a charm class definition: <bug-placeholder>

issue_title: Add support for chords
issue_body:
Running GW tasks in a chord, like so:
```
(celery.group(...) | other_task).delay()
```

Raises the following exception:
```
[2018-02-13 10:44:36,570: WARNING/ForkPoolWorker-4] Task celery.chord_unlock[25073a0a-0d2f-4de6-8183-7e6108a3497c] reject requeue=False: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'update'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/dan/.virtualenvs/gw/lib/python2.7/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 374, in trace_task
    R = retval = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/dan/projects/gw/girder_worker/girder_worker/task.py", line 151, in __call__
    results = super(Task, self).__call__(*_t_args, **_t_kwargs)
  File "/home/dan/.virtualenvs/gw/lib/python2.7/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 629, in __protected_call__
    return self.run(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/dan/.virtualenvs/gw/lib/python2.7/site-packages/celery/app/builtins.py", line 75, in unlock_chord
    raise self.retry(countdown=interval, max_retries=max_retries)
  File "/home/dan/.virtualenvs/gw/lib/python2.7/site-packages/celery/app/task.py", line 687, in retry
    try:
Reject: (AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'update'",), False)
```

This seems to be caused because our custom `apply_async` expects `options` to be a dict. However, when called via `chord_unlock` it's `None` because `chord_unlock` creates a signature and calls `apply_async` from there.

Perhaps Celery builtin tasks should be totally avoided by our code paths, we probably don't want to create jobs for various Celery housekeeping tasks, for example.

issue_title: Unintended collection deletion behaviour
issue_body:
``<Model>.objects.current.all().delete()`` does not apply the ``_delete_at()`` procedure defined in ``Versionable`` (which is to terminate each version), but deletes objects from the DB (including referencing objects; so, CASCADING is applied here). In this Django-default case, deletion is done via ``django.db.models.deletion.Collector``.
The current workaround is to do a for-loop over all instances and to call ``.delete()`` on each one of the instances. This terminates versions in the sense of CleanerVersion.

This issue is partially related to #42.

issue_title: More detailed error message about invalid json on python2
issue_body:
Hi. Loading invalid json file with datapackage.DataPackage will trow a DataPackageException:
```
>> dp = datapackage.DataPackage('/home/u1/datapackage.json')

  File "/home/u1/.virtualenvs/test-dpm/lib/python2.7/site-packages/datapackage/datapackage.py", line 56, in __init__
    self._descriptor = self._load_descriptor(descriptor)
  File "/home/u1/.virtualenvs/test-dpm/lib/python2.7/site-packages/datapackage/datapackage.py", line 312, in _load_descriptor
    six.raise_from(DataPackageException(msg), e)
  File "/home/u1/.virtualenvs/test-dpm/lib/python2.7/site-packages/six.py", line 718, in raise_from
    raise value
DataPackageException: Unable to load JSON at 'datapackage.json'

```
In the cli tool I would like to display more informative message about parsing error: the line and column where it happened. 

I can extract the original datailed parser error from json parser as `error.\__cause__` on python3. But can't do that on python2, because datapackge-py uses `six.raise_from()`, which forgets the original exception on python2.

Yes, one option would be to validate json inside our code before passing it to the datapackage.DataPackage(). But there is a desire to avoid this, and get detailed error message accessible from datapackage-py lib itself.
